concise:
Ayah 1–47 · linked video: 01. Adh-Dhariyat (Ayah 1-47)

Surah Adh-Dhariyat: The Winds That Scatter

Ayah 1

وَٱلذَّٰرِيَـٰتِ ذَرْوًا

This surah opens with a powerful oath: “By the winds that scatter.” The Arabic word الذَّٰرِيَـٰتِ refers to winds that blow and scatter dust, pollen, clouds and other elements across the earth. This imagery immediately introduces the life-giving quality of wind as a divine instrument of provision. These winds don’t merely move air—they scatter the very components of life itself throughout creation, distributing sustenance according to Allah’s ﷻ design.

Ayah 2

فَٱلْحَـٰمِلَـٰتِ وِقْرًا

The oath continues: “Then by those carrying heavy burdens.” These are the clouds laden with rain, carried by the winds mentioned in the previous ayah. The image is of enormous weight being transported with purpose—rain clouds moving across the sky, pollen being carried to fertilize plants, and all the heavy components necessary for sustaining life being moved from place to place.

Ayah 3

فَٱلْجَـٰرِيَـٰتِ يُسْرًا

“Then by those that flow with ease.” Despite carrying such heavy burdens, these winds and clouds move smoothly, flowing effortlessly across the sky. The word يُسْرًا emphasizes how naturally and gracefully these massive forces operate, highlighting the perfect system Allah ﷻ has established in creation.

Ayah 4

فَٱلْمُقَسِّمَـٰتِ أَمْرًا

“Then by those that distribute the command.” Here we see the divine distribution system at work. Allah ﷻ has decided which land receives wind, which receives rain, which receives the beneficial presence of pollinators like bees, and which remains barren. The word المُقَسِّمَـٰتِ reveals that there is a deliberate, calculated distribution of resources across the earth—a divine budget, if you will, where each region receives its appointed provision.

Ayah 5

إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَصَادِقٌ

After these four oaths, Allah ﷻ declares the conclusion: “Indeed, what you are promised is true.” The promise refers to the Day of Judgment, the resurrection, the accountability that awaits every soul. The emphatic particle لَصَادِقٌ affirms that this promise is absolutely truthful and certain.

Ayah 6

وَإِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ لَوَٰقِعٌ

“And indeed, the Judgment is about to occur.” The word الدِّينَ here refers to the Day of Recompense, and وَٰقِعٌ emphasizes its inevitability—it is not merely possible, it is certainly going to happen.

Ayah 7

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْحُبُكِ

Allah ﷻ swears again: “By the sky containing pathways.” The word الْحُبُكِ refers to the multiple pathways, tracks and roads that the celestial bodies follow. The stars, planets and heavenly bodies each move along their designated routes through the cosmos, creating a vast network of orbital paths above us.

Ayah 8

إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى قَوْلٍ مُّخْتَلِفٍ

“Indeed, you are in differing speech.” Just as the stars travel in different directions along various pathways, humanity holds vastly different views about life, death, purpose and the hereafter. Everyone has their own philosophy, their own take on reality. This diversity of opinion stands in stark contrast to the unified, purposeful movement of the heavens.

Ayah 9

يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ

“Deluded away from it is he who is deluded.” This profound statement reveals that those who turn away from the message of the Quran have already turned away from something else—the signs in creation itself. Someone who refuses to reflect on the winds, the sky, the earth and all the miraculous signs around them has already demonstrated a pattern of denial. When revelation comes, they simply continue their established habit of turning away from truth.

Ayah 10

قُتِلَ ٱلْخَرَّٰصُونَ

“Destroyed are the speculators”—or more literally, “cursed are those who make wild guesses.” While the previous ayah acknowledged that people have different opinions, this ayah specifically condemns those who speak about reality, the afterlife and the unseen realm based on nothing but conjecture, yet present their guesses as confident knowledge.

Ayah 11

ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ سَاهُونَ

“Those who are in flood, heedless.” The word غَمْرَةٍ suggests being overwhelmed, flooded or submerged. These individuals are so deeply immersed in their worldly concerns and distractions that they remain completely unaware of their obligations and the reality that awaits them.

Ayah 12

يَسْـَٔلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ

“They ask, ‘When is the Day of Recompense?’” The use of أَيَّانَ rather than the simpler مَتَىٰ indicates a tone of frustration or mockery in their questioning. They’re not asking out of genuine curiosity—they’re demanding proof as a challenge, essentially saying, “When is this supposed Day of Judgment you keep threatening us with?”

Ayah 13

يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ

The answer comes: “It is the Day they will be tormented over the Fire.” The word يُفْتَنُونَ comes from the root meaning to test or purify gold by melting it under intense heat. Just as gold is subjected to extreme temperatures to reveal its purity, these deniers will be subjected to the Fire.

Ayah 14

ذُوقُوا۟ فِتْنَتَكُمْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ

“Taste your torment! This is what you were impatient for.” They demanded to know when it would come, challenging its arrival—now they experience exactly what they rushed toward with their mockery.

Ayah 15

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ

After describing the fate of the deniers, Allah ﷻ presents the contrast: “Indeed, the righteous will be among gardens and springs.” The المُتَّقِينَ are those who sought to protect themselves from Allah’s ﷻ displeasure through consciousness and mindful living.

Ayah 16

ءَاخِذِينَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ

“Accepting what their Lord has given them. Indeed, they were before that doers of excellence.” They receive Allah’s ﷻ rewards gratefully, and this is a continuation of their character—they were always people of إِحْسَان , excellence in their worship and dealings, long before this reward came to them.

Ayah 17

كَانُوا۟ قَلِيلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مَا يَهْجَعُونَ

“They used to sleep but little of the night.” These believers didn’t spend their nights in complete rest and comfort. The phrase can be understood in multiple ways: either they slept very little, dedicating much of the night to worship, or during the nights when others engaged in sinful activities, they remained vigilant and avoided evil.

Ayah 18

وَبِٱلْأَسْحَارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ

“And in the hours before dawn, they would seek forgiveness.” The الْأَسْحَار refers to the pre-dawn hours, the time of tahajjud and spiritual preparation before Fajr. Even though these people avoided sin during the night, they still sought Allah’s ﷻ forgiveness in the morning. This reveals a beautiful spiritual consciousness—they worried they hadn’t done enough, that perhaps they had fallen short in some way, so they turned to istighfar as the sun rose.

Ayah 19

وَفِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ

“And in their wealth was a right for the one who asks and the one deprived.” Their spiritual excellence extended to their financial dealings. They recognized that a portion of their wealth was mandated—not as a favor or charity, but as a حَقٌّ , a right—for those who ask and those who suffer deprivation in silence.

Ayah 20

وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلْمُوقِنِينَ

“And in the earth are signs for those who are certain.” After describing the believers and their qualities, Allah ﷻ points to the evidence that supports faith—miraculous signs scattered throughout the earth for المُوقِنِينَ , those seeking conviction and certainty.

Ayah 21

وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ

“And in yourselves. Then will you not see?” The signs aren’t only external—they exist within our very being, in the miraculous nature of our own bodies, consciousness and existence. Why don’t people look carefully at themselves and recognize the divine design?

Ayah 22

وَفِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ

“And in the sky is your provision and whatever you are promised.” The sky contains both our worldly sustenance—rain, atmospheric conditions necessary for life—and the realities of the hereafter that await us.

Ayah 23

فَوَرَبِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُۥ لَحَقٌّ مِّثْلَ مَآ أَنَّكُمْ تَنطِقُونَ

“Then by the Lord of the heaven and earth, indeed it is truth, just as surely as you speak.” Allah ﷻ swears by Himself that the Quran is true. The comparison is powerful: just as real and certain as your own speech is to you when you converse with another person, that’s how real and certain the revelation is when the angel Jibreel (AS) speaks to the Prophet ﷺ. Your everyday conversations aren’t questioned—you know they’re real. The divine revelation is equally real.

Ayah 24

هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ

“Has there come to you the story of the honored guests of Ibrahim?” The connection to revelation continues—just as Prophet Muhammad ﷺ receives honored angelic visitors who bring divine messages, so too did Prophet Ibrahim (AS) receive honored guests. The word المُكْرَمِينَ indicates these weren’t ordinary visitors but dignified, honored beings.

Ayah 25

إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ

“When they entered upon him and said, ‘Peace.’ He answered, ‘Peace, unknown people.’” Notice the grammatical shift: they said سَلَـٰمًا, but Ibrahim (AS) responded with سَلَـٰمٌ . The change in vowel marking makes his greeting warmer, more welcoming—it’s the difference between a formal greeting and an enthusiastic “Welcome!” Yet even as he welcomed them, he acknowledged they were strangers: “You’re people I don’t recognize.”

Ayah 26

فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ فَجَآءَ بِعِجْلٍ سَمِينٍ

“Then he turned to his household and brought a fat calf.” Ibrahim (AS) didn’t ask permission or announce his intentions—فَرَاغَ suggests a subtle, swift movement. He quietly signaled to his wife, and soon brought forward an entire calf, well-fattened and prepared. سَمِينٍ can also mean well-roasted or fried—they prepared the whole animal, as was the generous custom of the time.

Ayah 27

فَقَرَّبَهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِمْ قَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ

“And he placed it near them. He said, ‘Will you not eat?’” He brought the food close to them, making it easily accessible, then gently invited them to partake. This wasn’t a casual “I already ate” situation—they didn’t touch the food at all.

Ayah 28

فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ وَبَشَّرُوهُ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ

“And he felt from them apprehension. They said, ‘Fear not,’ and gave him good tidings of a learned son.” There was an ancient code of honor: assassins wouldn’t eat your food before killing you, as accepting someone’s hospitality created an obligation they couldn’t violate while intending harm. When Ibrahim (AS) saw his guests refusing to eat, fear crept into his heart. They immediately reassured him and then delivered remarkable news—he would have a son who would grow up to be عَلِيمٍ (alim), a man of profound knowledge. This refers to Ishaq (AS).

Ayah 29

فَأَقْبَلَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ فِى صَرَّةٍ فَصَكَّتْ وَجْهَهَا وَقَالَتْ عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ

“And his wife came forward with a loud cry, and struck her face, saying, ‘An old barren woman!’” Sarah had been in another part of the home when she heard this announcement. Other narrations mention that she first laughed at the absurdity of it, then came forward in astonishment. فَصَكَّتْ وَجْهَهَا describes her striking her own face in shock. She protested: I’m عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ —an old woman who has never been able to bear children. How is this possible?

Ayah 30

قَالُوا۟ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

“They said, ‘Thus has your Lord spoken. Indeed, He is the Wise, the Knowing.’” The angels affirmed that this was Allah’s ﷻ decree. He is الْحَكِيمُ —the perfectly Wise in all His decisions—and الْعَلِيمُ —the All-Knowing who comprehends every detail of every situation.

Ayah 31

۞ قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ

“He said, ‘Then what is your business, O messengers?’” Ibrahim (AS), with his perceptive wisdom, recognized that angels don’t visit just to announce a birth. There must be another, more serious purpose for their arrival.

Ayah 32

قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ

“They said, ‘Indeed, we have been sent to a people of criminals.’” They confirmed his suspicion—their primary mission was to go to a nation steeped in crime and transgression.

Ayah 33

لِنُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن طِينٍ

“To send down upon them stones of clay.” The حِجَارَةً مِّن طِينٍ refers to stones formed from dried, compacted clay—what we might call petrified mud or hardened sediment. The Arabic word سِجِّيل , mentioned in other passages, derives from this concept.

Ayah 34

مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ

“Marked from your Lord for the transgressors.” These stones weren’t random—they were مُّسَوَّمَةً , branded or marked with the names of their intended targets. Each stone had a specific destination, assigned by Allah ﷻ for المُسْرِفِينَ , those who had gone far beyond acceptable boundaries in their transgression.

Ayah 35

فَأَخْرَجْنَا مَن كَانَ فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

“So We brought out whoever was in it of the believers.” Before the punishment fell, Allah ﷻ extracted all the believers from the doomed cities.

Ayah 36

فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ

“And We found not within them other than a house of Muslims.” The tragic reality—in that entire region, only one household submitted to Allah ﷻ: the home of Lut (AS).

Ayah 37

وَتَرَكْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ

“And We left therein a sign for those who fear the painful punishment.” The ruins were left as a permanent reminder. Interestingly, the archaeological site known as the “drowned cities” wasn’t discovered until the 1980s, long after the Quran described these events—yet another sign for those who reflect.

Ayah 38

وَفِى مُوسَىٰٓ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ

“And in Musa, when We sent him to Fir’awn with clear authority.” The pattern continues—another prophet sent with undeniable proof, a سُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ , clear and compelling evidence of divine appointment.

Ayah 39

فَتَوَلَّىٰ بِرُكْنِهِۦ وَقَالَ سَـٰحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ

“But he turned away with his supporters and said, ‘A magician or a madman.’” The word بِرُكْنِهِۦ can mean “with his pillar” or “with his strength”—his army, his support system, his base of power. Despite clear signs, Fir’awn dismissed Musa (AS) as either a magician or insane.

Ayah 40

فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ وَجُنُودَهُۥ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ

“So We seized him and his soldiers and cast them into the sea, and he was blameworthy.” Notice the emphasis: أَخَذْنَـٰهُ —We seized him specifically—وَجُنُودَهُۥ —and his army. His capture was distinct, marked. Then they were all فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ thrown, cast away into the sea. وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ —and he was full of blame, regret and humiliation.

Ayah 41

وَفِى عَادٍ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلرِّيحَ ٱلْعَقِيمَ

“And in ’Ad, when We sent against them the barren wind.” We return to the theme of wind that opened the surah. While the beginning described winds that scatter life and provision, here we encounter الرِّيحَ ٱلْعَقِيمَ—wind that is sterile, barren, bringing no life, no rain, no benefit. Just as Sarah described herself as عَقِيمٌ —barren—this wind carried no capacity for nurturing life. The word is feminine by itself, so no need to add the feminine marker.

Ayah 42

مَا تَذَرُ مِن شَىْءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا جَعَلَتْهُ كَٱلرَّمِيمِ

“It left nothing that it came upon except that it made it like disintegrated ruins.” الرَّمِيمِ refers to crushed, deteriorated matter—like ancient bones that have decomposed to the point where they crumble at the slightest touch. This wind pulverized everything in its path, leaving only fragile remnants that collapsed into dust.

Ayah 43

وَفِى ثَمُودَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ

“And in Thamud, when it was said to them, ‘Enjoy yourselves for a limited time.’” They were given a brief respite, a final opportunity before the punishment arrived—حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ , until an appointed time.

Ayah 44

فَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ وَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ

“But they rebelled against the command of their Lord, so the thunderbolt seized them while they were looking on.” عَتَوْا۟ indicates serious, arrogant rebellion—not mere disobedience but defiant transgression. The الصَّـٰعِقَةُ , a devastating thunderbolt or explosion, struck them down while they watched, conscious and aware of what was happening to them.

Ayah 45

فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مِن قِيَامٍ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُنتَصِرِينَ

“And they were unable to rise, nor could they defend themselves.” They couldn’t even stand up to flee, much less fight back or call for help. Complete, utter helplessness overtook them.

Ayah 46

وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ

“And the people of Nuh before them—indeed, they were a defiantly disobedient people.” The surah concludes its survey of destroyed nations by reaching back to the earliest example: the people of Nuh (AS), who were فَـٰسِقِينَ —deeply corrupt and rebellious in their disobedience.

Ayah 47

وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا بِأَيْي۟دٍ وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ

“And the heaven We constructed with strength, and indeed, We are expanding it.” After surveying the destruction of past nations, the surah returns to the cosmic scale. Allah ﷻ declares that He built the heavens بِأَيْي۟دٍ —with power, with hands, with might—and then adds a remarkable statement: وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ —We are continuously expanding it.

This declaration speaks to the expanding universe, a reality only confirmed by modern science. The ongoing expansion—لَمُوسِعُونَ uses a present, continuous form—means Allah ﷻ isn’t just describing a past act of creation but an ongoing process. For some people, encountering this single scientific insight in the Quran becomes a doorway to faith. They ask themselves: How could someone 1,400 years ago, without telescopes or modern instruments, know that the universe is constantly expanding? Where could this knowledge have come from? For such individuals, the mind recognizes truth first, and the heart follows. This ayah demonstrates how Allah ﷻ addresses humanity through multiple pathways—some through spiritual reflection, others through intellectual investigation—all leading to the same destination: recognition of the Creator.

Ayah 47–60 · linked video: 02. Adh-Dhariyat (Ayah 47-60)

Surah Adh-Dhariyat: Ayahs 47-60

Ayah 47

وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا بِأَيْي۟دٍ وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ

“And the sky - We built it with power, and indeed, We are expanding it.”

The construction of this ayah reveals something profound about how Allah ﷻ draws attention to His creation. The Arabic structure here is unique: وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا - “the sky, We built it.” Notice how the object is mentioned, then the verb, then the pronoun refers back to that object again. This repetition serves a specific purpose - it magnifies the accomplishment by making you focus on both the achievement and the One who achieved it.

When someone wants to highlight the remarkable nature of what they’ve done, they mention their accomplishment twice: “This thing here? I did that. Yes, that very thing.” This is what Allah ﷻ does here with the sky. The heavens are no small thing - We are the ones who built it.

The phrase بِأَيْي۟دٍ - “with power” or “with hands” - emphasizes the might behind this creation. In English, we use similar expressions: “I built this with my own hands” or “I did this on my own” to convey personal power and capability. This is how Allah ﷻ establishes His creative power.

Then comes the remarkable declaration: وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ - “and indeed, We are expanding it.” The universe isn’t static; Allah ﷻ keeps expanding it constantly. This isn’t just about the initial act of creation, but the ongoing expansion of the cosmos.

Ayah 48

وَٱلْأَرْضَ فَرَشْنَـٰهَا فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَـٰهِدُونَ

“And the earth - We spread it out, and how excellent are We at preparing it!”

After speaking about the sky, Allah ﷻ turns to the earth: وَٱلْأَرْضَ فَرَشْنَـٰهَا - “the earth, We furnished it.” The word فَرَشْنَـٰهَا carries the meaning of spreading something out, making it like comfortable flooring, a carpet, or bedding. It’s the same root used for فِرَاش - a mattress or comfortable place to rest.

فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَـٰهِدُونَ - “how excellent are We at making it smooth!” The word مَـٰهِدُونَ comes from the same root as مَهْد , which means cradle. It’s where we get the concept of a mother’s gentle care - مَاهِد, one who makes things comfortable and smooth for a child. Allah ﷻ made the earth smooth, relaxing, a place where you can find comfort and stability.

These two creations - the sky and the earth - complement one another perfectly. They work hand in hand. The earth’s functions cannot be fulfilled without the sky, and the sky was created in a way that services the needs of the earth. This pairing sets up what comes next.

Ayah 49

وَمِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ

“And of all things We created pairs, so that you may remember.”

وَمِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَا زَوْجَيْنِ - “of all things We created pairs.” Everything exists in pairs: the sky and the earth, male and female, night and day, sun and moon. This is a fundamental pattern woven into creation.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ - “so that you may take advice, so that you may remember.” But what’s the benefit of reflecting on this pairing? What lesson should we draw?

If everything was created in pairs, and human beings were created in pairs, and life itself exists in dualities, then why don’t you realize that this worldly life has its pair too? There is another life coming - the Hereafter. Just as everything around you has its complement, this temporal existence has its eternal counterpart. The pattern of pairs should wake you up to the reality of resurrection and the Day of Judgment.

Ayah 50

فَفِرُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

“So flee to Allah. Indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner.”

فَفِرُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ - “then run to Allah!” This is an urgent command. On the Day of Judgment, people will be running toward Allah ﷻ whether they like it or not. They’ll have no choice. But now? Now is the time to run to Him voluntarily. Don’t wait until you’re forced.

إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ - “Indeed, I am especially for you, on behalf of Him, a warner making things very clear.” The Prophet ﷺ is speaking here, declaring his role. مِّنْهُ - “from Him,” meaning on behalf of Allah ﷻ. نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ - a warner whose message is crystal clear, leaving no room for confusion.

Ayah 51

وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ

“And do not make another deity with Allah. Indeed, I am to you from Him a clear warner.”

وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ - “And don’t place any other gods alongside Allah.” Don’t set up other entities of worship, other beings you obey instead of Him. This is the core message.

The ayah concludes with the same phrase: إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ - “I am giving you clear warning. I’ve come especially on His behalf to give you clear warning.” The repetition emphasizes the urgency and seriousness of the message.

Ayah 52

كَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَتَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا قَالُوا۟ سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ

“Similarly, no messenger came to those before them except that they said, ‘A magician or a madman.’”

كَذَٰلِكَ - “This is just the same.” It’s always been this way. This is the recurring narrative of history. مَآ أَتَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ - not a single messenger came to nations before them, إِلَّا قَالُوا۟ سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ - except that they said the same thing over and over again: “He’s a magician!” or “No, he’s insane! He’s possessed!”

Every messenger faced this accusation. Every single one heard these labels. It’s the same pattern repeating throughout history.

Ayah 53

أَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ

“Did they advise each other to this? Rather, they are a transgressing people.”

أَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِهِۦ - “Have they given advice to each other about this?” The word تَوَاصَوْا۟ comes from وَصِيَّة - a will, advice passed down from one generation to the next. We know this concept well: وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ - “advise each other to truth and patience.”

This is like what Ibrahim (AS) did at his deathbed, and what Yaqub (AS) did at his. When Yaqub (AS) was dying, he gathered his sons: أَمْ كُنتُمْ شُهَدَآءَ إِذْ حَضَرَ يَعْقُوبَ ٱلْمَوْتُ - “Were you witnesses when death approached Yaqub?” He left them good advice, and this advice was passed down through multiple generations.

Even Yusuf (AS) understood this legacy. When he was in prison, he reminded the inmates of the wasiyyah that came through his father, grandfather and great-grandfather - going back to Ibrahim (AS): إِنِّى تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ - “I have left the religion of a people who don’t believe in Allah.” He spoke of being on the same legacy, the same path. This was wasiyyah - good advice passed down generation to generation.

Now comes the irony: generation after generation kept doing the same evil to the messengers. They kept calling them magicians and kept calling them insane. Is this the wasiyyah being passed down? Is this evil advice traveling from one nation to the next?

In some sense, yes. The same Shaytan who whispered to previous nations leaves that legacy behind. “Oh, that worked with the previous messenger? Might as well corrupt this new nation too and get them to say the same thing.” So just as truth is constant, the forces against truth are also constant. They’ve been the same throughout history.

بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ - “Rather, the fact of the matter is they are a rebellious, transgressing nation.”

Ayah 54

فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَمَآ أَنتَ بِمَلُومٍ

“So turn away from them, for you are not to be blamed.”

فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ - “Then turn away from them.” Those kinds of people bent upon character assassination, calling you a magician, calling you insane, refusing to listen to what you’re actually saying - just turn away from them.

فَمَآ أَنتَ بِمَلُومٍ - “You are not blameworthy. You are not to be blamed at all.” The word مَلُومٍ comes from لَوْم - to blame someone. You won’t be blamed for walking away from them.

This is important. When you talk to somebody and instead of listening to what you have to say, they start attacking your character or slapping labels on you, that means they’re not ready to talk. They’re not ready to listen. They’re not ready to engage their intellect. At that point, there’s no point wasting your time with people like that.

The Prophet ﷺ worried about this. If he turns away from people, he doesn’t know if Allah ﷻ will ask him: “Why did you walk away from that conversation? Why didn’t you keep trying?” So Allah ﷻ lets him off the hook and says, “No, you can turn away. You’re not going to be blamed. That’s all on them.”

Ayah 55

وَذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

“And remind, for indeed, the reminder benefits the believers.”

وَذَكِّرْ - “And keep on reminding.” Now, a reminder is different from a conversation. A reminder is one-way. I’m trying to remind you, and whatever you say in response - well, think about an alarm clock. When it goes off, you can yell at it. You can give it a death glare. You can hate it and want to slam it into the wall. But the alarm clock is just going to do what it’s going to do, whether you get angry or not. It’s just there to wake you up. It’s there to remind you.

In some sense, the messenger has to develop a very thick skin. He has to keep on reminding, no matter what the reaction of the people is. How demoralizing is it for a speaker to be heckled? To have people start walking away as he’s talking? It’s deeply deflating. You start questioning yourself: “Am I saying something wrong? Why are people doing this?”

It’s insulting. People did this to the Prophet ﷺ all the time. And it’s not just one or two people walking away - that you can handle, you can call them back - but when everybody starts leaving? That’s devastating.

Allah ﷻ says you need to learn not to be taken in by their reactions. You just keep reminding. فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ - yes, turn away from them. But when you do come to remind them and they turn away, don’t get disheartened. Just keep going.

فَإِنَّ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “for indeed, the reminder is going to benefit those who truly have iman, the truest believers.”

You don’t know who in that crowd has the seeds of iman. When the Prophet ﷺ addresses all of Makkah, he doesn’t know yet who the Rumanas are, who the Abu Bakrs are, who the Umars (RA) are. He doesn’t know who is going to emerge and show their iman, because it’s all hidden inside people’s hearts.

Allah ﷻ just says to him: You keep on reminding.

This applies to us too. Sometimes you have an opportunity to say something in a crowd that’s not particularly religious - and I’m not even talking about non-Muslim audiences (though that’s also a great opportunity). Even within Muslim ranks, Islamic society has changed. It’s not like it was before.

Back in the day, people used to hide their disobedience to Allah ﷻ. When it was an Islamic society, people concealed their sins. There were people who missed prayer, people who drank alcohol, people who were promiscuous or had bad character. These things existed, but they weren’t out in public. They were behind closed doors.

Now we’re living in a different time. Muslims in Western societies and Muslims all over the world - their disobedience to Allah ﷻ is no longer behind closed doors. It’s celebrated. It’s on billboards. It’s at parties, at open gatherings, at university campuses, at workplaces. It’s open. People who take bribes don’t hide it anymore. In Muslim countries, they’ll hand you your passport back and say, “It’s a little light” - meaning you need to put cash in there before giving it back. They’re open about their bribery. There’s no shame left.

When you see people like that, it’s easy to think: these people are closer to what Banu Israil became. They were out there openly violating the Sabbath, fishing, leaving nets, digging holes for water so fish would get trapped. Those people in that story are goners. What’s the point of even talking to them? Those are the “liberals,” the ones openly sinning - why bother?

The answer is: yes, go talk to them about Islam. Because you don’t know who has iman inside them. You have to talk to everybody. You can’t just say, “I’m going to address the religious crowd. I’m going to preach to the choir.” It doesn’t work like that.

You have to go to the crowd that’s unwilling to listen. All of our prophets - the legacy they left - was to talk predominantly to the unwilling audience. Our Messenger ﷺ had the added advantage of that core group of followers who eventually became a community. He addressed the willing, though even among them there were problematic elements - but those had to be dealt with carefully.

Our situation today is much closer to what some prophets faced with Banu Israil. لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْمًا ٱللَّهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ - “Why are you bothering to advise a group of people that Allah ﷻ has already decided to destroy?” That’s what some people said. “Why are you even bothering? These guys are fuel for hell. Don’t waste your time, brother. Don’t waste your time, sister. Don’t go talk to these people. They don’t need it. They don’t want to hear your speech.”

You don’t know that. Sure, maybe a lot of them don’t want to hear your speech. Maybe many will heckle you and call you names. But some of them - Allah ﷻ knows who has iman inside. You don’t get to judge that.

فَإِنَّ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - the reminder will truly benefit those who really, truly believe.

One last point: Someone could listen to hours and hours of speech, and nothing enters their heart. Having words enter the heart is not up to a human being - that’s Allah’s doing. Then there could be a person who speaks to someone else for just one minute, thirty seconds even, and Allah ﷻ decides that the words coming out of your mouth, He’s going to give them power. He’ll make them pierce right through that person’s heart and change their life. They’ll attribute their entire transformation to that one conversation.

We don’t know which of our words Allah ﷻ will empower. We don’t know which words He’ll allow to enter somebody’s heart and which words He’ll keep blocked because they don’t deserve it yet, or they’re not ready yet. That’s up to Allah ﷻ. So you keep on reminding, because you don’t know. You don’t get to judge what’s inside someone.

Ayah 56

وَمَا خَلَقْتُ ٱلْجِنَّ وَٱلْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ

“And I did not create the jinn and mankind except to worship Me.”

وَمَا خَلَقْتُ ٱلْجِنَّ وَٱلْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ - “I didn’t create the jinn and human beings with any other purpose except that they should enslave themselves to Me.”

Look at the placement of this ayah. It’s so commonly quoted, and yes, human beings do have a purpose. But notice: jinn are mentioned first, created first, and they’re mentioned first here too. Jinn are also the obstacle for most human beings in taking in the message.

The fact that Allah ﷻ mentions this - that this is the purpose for which you were created, and that’s what the messenger is trying to remind you of - addresses a deep frustration. It’s frustrating to see people completely ignoring Allah’s laws, violating Allah’s expectations. Their attitudes don’t reflect the purpose of their life at all.

It’s disheartening, especially when you see it with people close to you - your own family, your own friends. It bothers a person deeply. Usually, the remedy people find is: “I just won’t deal with them. I won’t even bother.”

But Allah ﷻ says: whether they accept it or not, that is the purpose for which they were created.

Ayah 57

مَآ أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ

“I do not want from them any provision, nor do I want them to feed Me.”

مَآ أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ - “I don’t ask for any provisions from them. I’m not asking for anything.” وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ - “And I’m not intending that they should feed Me somehow.”

Allah ﷻ needs nothing from His creation. This declaration removes any notion that worship is for Allah’s benefit. He doesn’t need our prayers, our fasting, our charity. These acts are entirely for our benefit.

Ayah 58

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلرَّزَّاقُ ذُو ٱلْقُوَّةِ ٱلْمَتِينُ

“Indeed, it is Allah who is the Provider, the Possessor of Power, the Strong.”

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلرَّزَّاقُ - “No doubt, Allah - He in fact is the One who continuously, repeatedly keeps on providing.” Even the disbeliever is provided for. The Muslim guy who’s a millionaire with a bar inside his house, involved in all kinds of haram - who’s giving him his money? Where is it coming from? Allah ﷻ is the One.

ذُو ٱلْقُوَّةِ ٱلْمَتِينُ - “the Possessor of relentless, extended power.” The word مَتِينُ comes from a root meaning something extended, like a long rope. Allah’s power is extended, unbreakable, reaching everywhere and everything.

Ayah 59

فَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذَنُوبًا مِّثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحَـٰبِهِمْ فَلَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونِ

“For indeed, those who have wronged will have a share [of punishment] like the share of their companions, so let them not urge Me on.”

فَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذَنُوبًا - “Then for absolutely certain, those who have done wrong, they have a dhanub.”

Now, what’s a dhanub? It sounds like ذَنْب - sin - so some translations might render it as “sin.” But that’s not quite right.

A ذَنُوب is actually a word used in Arabic for a bucket that’s used to draw water from a well. It’s not just any bucket - it’s specifically the bucket lowered into a well to draw water out. It has a rope - حَبْل - attached to it, called a رِشَاء .

When there’s a lot of water, the bucket fills up completely and becomes heavy, so you have to pull it out slowly, carefully, step by step. As you’re pulling, you’re working hard, hoping things will get better. You’re anticipating: “I’m finally going to get what I want. I’m finally going to drink.”

This drawing water from a well was a common Arabic practice. The imagery here is profound: these sins and wrongdoings that people commit - the efforts they make every day, the schemes they pursue, the investments in haram, the plans for worldly pleasure - they think they’re drawing something beneficial up from the well.

لَكِنَّ ٱلذَّنُوبَ سَبَبُ الْمَالِ وَٱلْجَزَاءِ عَلَيْهِمْ - But what they’re drawing out will be the exact opposite of what they’re expecting. They’re putting in all these efforts, seeing results, making progress day after day. They have expectations and projections about how things will turn out. “These haram investments will pay off. These parties, these plans - they’re going to work to my advantage. I’ll experience great pleasure, great joy.”

But Allah ﷻ says no. When the bucket finally rises up, they won’t find water in it - they’ll find fire.

مِّثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحَـٰبِهِمْ - “just like the dhanub of their companions.” Who are these companions? The people who came many generations ago. They’re أَصْحَاب - companions in practice. The people of Fir’awn, the people of ’Ad, the people of Thamud - these are companions in practice and methodology.

فَلَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونِ - “then they shouldn’t rush it. They shouldn’t try to pull the water out too quickly.” Take your time. The punishment is coming.

Ayah 60

فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن يَوْمِهِمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ

“Then woe to those who disbelieved from their Day which they are promised.”

فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ - “Then terrible destruction, the worst destruction falls upon those who disbelieved.”

مِن يَوْمِهِمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ - “from a Day, from their Day that they have been promised.”

The surah concludes with this stark warning. The Day is coming. It’s been promised. And for those who rejected the message, who turned away from the reminder, who mocked the messengers and persisted in their wrongdoing - that Day will bring them not the reward they imagined, but destruction beyond measure.
